10.objective: Train a GPT-2 (124M) language model from scratch using raw PyTorch, achieving a final training loss below 3.5 after 5,000+ steps.

steps.title: Set up environment
steps.description: Verify your GPU is available and import the required libraries. You need PyTorch with CUDA support and a dataset (we'll use a small text corpus).
steps.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.nn import functional as F

print(f'CUDA available: {torch.cuda.is_available()}')
print(f'Device: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)}')
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
steps.hint: If CUDA is not available, check your PyTorch installation: pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

steps.title: Define model architecture
steps.description: Implement a GPT-2 style transformer with token embeddings, positional embeddings, transformer blocks (attention + MLP), and a language model head.
steps.code:
class GPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, n_embd=768, n_head=12,
                 n_layer=12, block_size=1024):
        super().__init__()
        self.tok_emb = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, n_embd)
        self.pos_emb = nn.Embedding(block_size, n_embd)
        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            *[Block(n_embd, n_head) for _ in range(n_layer)]
        )
        self.ln_f = nn.LayerNorm(n_embd)
        self.head = nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size, bias=False)

    def forward(self, idx, targets=None):
        B, T = idx.shape
        tok = self.tok_emb(idx)
        pos = self.pos_emb(torch.arange(T, device=idx.device))
        x = self.blocks(tok + pos)
        logits = self.head(self.ln_f(x))
        loss = None
        if targets is not None:
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.view(-1, logits.size(-1)),
                targets.view(-1)
            )
        return logits, loss
steps.hint: The Block class needs multi-head self-attention + MLP with GELU activation. Don't forget the residual connections: x = x + attn(x) and x = x + mlp(x).

steps.title: Write the training loop
steps.description: Implement the standard training loop: forward pass → compute loss → backward pass → optimizer step. Log the loss every 100 steps. Use AdamW optimizer with learning rate 3e-4.
steps.code:
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)

for step in range(max_steps):
    xb, yb = get_batch('train')  # your data loader

    # Forward pass
    logits, loss = model(xb, yb)

    # Backward pass
    optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

    # Log every 100 steps
    if step % 100 == 0:
        print(f'step {step}: loss {loss.item():.4f}')
steps.hint: Call optimizer.zero_grad() BEFORE loss.backward(), not after. Use set_to_none=True for a small performance boost. If loss doesn't decrease, check your learning rate.

steps.title: Record your results
steps.description: Run training for at least 5,000 steps. Record your final training loss and tokens/sec throughput. Compare against the targets below.
steps.code:
# After training:
print(f'Final loss: {loss.item():.4f}')

# Measure throughput:
import time
torch.cuda.synchronize()
t0 = time.time()
for _ in range(100):
    logits, loss = model(xb, yb)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
torch.cuda.synchronize()
dt = time.time() - t0
tokens_per_sec = 100 * xb.numel() / dt
print(f'Throughput: {tokens_per_sec:.0f} tokens/sec')
steps.hint: Loss should decrease to ~3.0–3.5 after 5K steps on a small corpus. If it's stuck above 4.0, your attention mask or positional embeddings may be wrong.

11.objective: Read and annotate every line of a PyTorch training loop until you can modify it (change optimizer, add gradient clipping, swap scheduler) without consulting docs.

steps.title: Read the training loop line by line
steps.description: Open your training script from the previous lab. For each line, write a comment explaining exactly what it does and why.
steps.code:
# What does this line do? Why set_to_none=True?
optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)

# What happens inside loss.backward()?
# Which parameters get gradients? How?
loss.backward()

# What does optimizer.step() actually change?
# How does AdamW differ from SGD here?
optimizer.step()
steps.hint: zero_grad(set_to_none=True) sets .grad to None instead of zeroing the tensor — saves a memset operation. Small optimization, but shows you understand the internals.

steps.title: Modify the optimizer
steps.description: Replace AdamW with SGD with momentum. Run training and compare convergence speed.
steps.code:
# Original:
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)

# Replace with:
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=1e-2,  # SGD needs higher LR
    momentum=0.9
)
steps.hint: SGD will converge slower than AdamW on transformer training. You may need 10x the steps to reach the same loss.

steps.title: Add gradient clipping
steps.description: Add gradient norm clipping after backward pass, before optimizer step. This prevents gradient explosion.
steps.code:
loss.backward()

# Clip gradient norm to 1.0
torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(
    model.parameters(),
    max_norm=1.0
)

optimizer.step()
steps.hint: Gradient clipping is almost always used in transformer training. The max_norm value of 1.0 is standard. Monitor grad norms to see if clipping activates.

steps.title: Add a learning rate scheduler
steps.description: Implement cosine annealing with warmup. This is the standard scheduler for LLM training.
steps.hint: Warmup prevents early training instability. Cosine decay helps the model converge to a sharper minimum. Most LLM papers use this exact schedule.
